text: The surgeon places trocars in the left upper quadrant, right lower quadrant, and umbilicus for a three-port robotic cholecystectomy and liver biopsy, preparing for gallbladder extraction.
I found that the ICG fluorescence dye and the Firefly tend to really help delineate the anatomy and making my dissection much more comfortable and safe.
The surgeon uses gentle traction on the gallbladder to identify the cystic duct, visualizing the critical view of safety enhanced by the ICG fluorescence dye, which has been metabolized in the bile.

The surgeon skeletonizes the cystic duct and artery, creating space for the robotic clips. Once the anatomy is clearly visualized and the skeletalization is complete, three clips are placed on the cystic duct.

The surgeon uses robotic scissors to ensure an adequate seal before cutting, carefully verifying the absence of aberrant gallbladder anatomy or cystic duct. The common duct is confirmed to be distant from the area where the gallbladder is being removed from the liver fossa.

The surgeon uses robotic scissors to remove the gallbladder from the liver bed, with minimal bleeding observed. The liver appears fatty, consistent with the patient's history of abnormal liver functions.
After the gallbladder is safely removed from the liver bed with excellent hemostasis, the robotic instruments are used to take a small biopsy for diagnostic purposes.

So again, this procedure can be done with only one arm without an assistant. I'm retracting for myself using my left arm. We find that a progress tends to have a good amount of tension on the gallbladder. And we do have to maneuver a couple times to get the retraction that you need. But overall, with time, this could be a very effective technique. It's also decreasing the cost of the case by avoiding a fourth arm.

The robotic system is used to take a small liver biopsy from a section of the liver. The biopsy is carefully extracted without cauterization to ensure the tissue remains suitable for pathological examination.

The surgeon removes the gallbladder, placing it in a ten millimeter bag through the initial eight millimeter trocar site to prevent spillage or contamination of gallstones. The gallbladder is extracted through the umbilical port, and the trocars are closed using a Cutter Thompson and Xeo-Vicro suture to prevent hernias.
